draft.introduction: Part Two of the Popol Vuh describes the failed attempts to create humanity. The gods first try mud, then wood, before finally succeeding with corn—establishing maize as sacred to Maya identity.

translation.section: Part Two: The Creation of Humans
translation.text: First the Maker and Modeler formed a creature of mud, but it dissolved and could not speak. They destroyed it. Then they carved people from wood, and these walked and spoke, but they had no hearts and no minds. They did not remember their creator. A great flood was sent to destroy them. At last the gods found white and yellow corn, and from this they ground the flesh of the first true humans. These four men could see everything; their vision reached to the edges of the world. But the gods were troubled: "Must they be equal to us?" So they breathed mist upon their eyes and limited their sight.

translator_notes.note: The gods' decision to limit human vision—making the first humans less than divine—parallels the Prometheus myth and the Eden narrative, in which divine beings restrict human access to knowledge or immortality. The identification of human flesh with corn is not merely metaphorical for the Maya: maize agriculture was literally the foundation of their civilization, and this narrative sacralizes that dependence.
